title: Install the toolchain and learn what uploading really does
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Install the Arduino IDE, add ESP32-S3 support, and get your computer to see the board. While it installs, learn what actually happens when you press Upload — compile, download mode, flash, run — so that when something goes wrong on a later day you can reason about where in that chain it broke instead of guessing.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

coachInstructions: Walk the install one step at a time; this is the fiddliest day of the course.
Use the install wait to teach the upload chain- compile, enter download mode, write to flash, reset and run.
Frame every problem by its stage- no port means the serial bridge (driver or cable); board missing from the list means the compile target (board package) isn't installed.
If no port appears, check the CH343 driver, then a data-capable cable, then board and port selection, in that order.
There's no code to upload today — the board appearing as a port is the success condition, and it's a one-time cost.
steps: Download and install Arduino IDE 2 from https://www.arduino.cc/en/software/, then open it.
Open Arduino IDE's settings. On macOS choose Arduino IDE → Settings… or press ⌘,. On Windows or Linux choose File → Preferences.

Scroll to Additional boards manager URLs near the bottom. Click the small button at the right of that field, paste https://espressif.github.io/arduino-esp32/package_esp32_index.json on its own line, then click OK twice. This address tells Arduino IDE where Espressif publishes ESP32 support.

Open Tools → Board → Boards Manager… (or click the board-shaped icon in the left sidebar). Search for esp32, find esp32 by Espressif Systems, click Install, and wait until the IDE says the platform is installed. Espressif's install guide at https://docs.espressif.com/projects/arduino-esp32/en/latest/installing.html describes the same package.
Plug the board directly into the computer with a data-capable USB-C cable. A charge-only cable can light the board up but cannot create a connection in Arduino IDE.

Identify the board's port instead of guessing. Open the board selector at the top of Arduino IDE and note the list. Unplug the ESP32-S3, wait a few seconds, then plug it back in. The item that disappears and returns is the board's port; on macOS its name usually contains usbserial or usbmodem. Ignore Bluetooth, headphones, and debug-console entries.

Select that returning port. If Arduino IDE calls it Unknown, choose Select other board and port…, select the returning port on the right, search for and select ESP32S3 Dev Module on the left, then click OK. The board choice tells the IDE what chip to compile for; the port tells it which connected device should receive the program.

If no item disappears and returns, do not guess from the list. First try another known data-capable USB cable and a different USB port. If the board is still absent, install the CH343 USB driver from https://github.com/Freenove/Freenove_Super_Starter_Kit_for_ESP32_S3/tree/main/CH343, restart the computer if its installer asks, and reconnect the board.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Compile your code for this chip, drop the board into download mode, write it to flash, then run it.

notes.title: Why there's a driver and a "port"
notes.body: The board's bootloader speaks old-fashioned serial; your computer speaks USB. A translator chip bridges them, which is why the board shows up as a port and why some systems need its driver first.

notes.title: Board vs port, made concrete
notes.body: Board is a compile-time choice — which chip's instructions to build. Port is a delivery choice — which connected device to send them to. Both must be right, for different reasons.

expectedOutputExample: Your ESP32-S3 appears as a selectable port in Tools → Port.
The board name is selectable under Tools → Board.
test.successCriteria: Seeing the port is the whole win today — that port is the serial bridge your code will travel across on Day 3.

firstChecks: Unplug the board and watch the port list, then reconnect it. If nothing new appears, try a known data-capable cable and a different USB port before installing the CH343 driver from https://github.com/Freenove/Freenove_Super_Starter_Kit_for_ESP32_S3/tree/main/CH343.

troubleshooting.symptom: Only a charging sound, no port
firstChecks: The cable carries power but no data — swap in a data-capable USB-C cable.

troubleshooting.symptom: Board name missing from the list
firstChecks: Re-open Boards Manager and confirm esp32 by Espressif Systems finished installing. Then use Select other board and port… and search for ESP32S3 Dev Module.

challenge: List your computer's serial devices with the board unplugged, plug it in, list again to see the new device appear, then unplug to watch it vanish.
